content:
# Ephesians Chapter 5 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

### v.2 — "offering and sacrifice" vs. "sacrifice for us" — sacrificial formula

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "an offering and a sacrifice to God for a fragrant aroma" | prosphoran kai thysian tō theō eis osmēn euōdias |
| LOGOS | "a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God" | Combines — "fragrant" modifies "offering" |
| PNEUMA | "a sacrifice for us, a pleasing aroma to God" | "For us"; "pleasing aroma" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek has "an offering and sacrifice to God, for a fragrant aroma" — the aroma phrase modifies the whole. LOGOS's "fragrant offering and sacrifice" is slightly compressed but accurate. PNEUMA adds "for us" to the sacrifice clause (already present earlier in the verse) and renders osmēn euōdias as "pleasing aroma" — adequate but loses the LXX formula.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — word arrangement.

---

### v.5 — "covetous...an idolater" structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "every sexually immoral or impure or greedy [person], who is an idolater" | ho estin eidōlolatrēs — relative clause |
| LOGOS | "who is covetous (that is, an idolater)" | Parenthetical — "that is" |
| PNEUMA | "For a greedy person is an idolater, worshiping the things of this world" | Adds "worshiping the things of this world" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek identifies the covetous person as an idolater (ho estin eidōlolatrēs). PNEUMA adds "worshiping the things of this world" — an interpretive expansion not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds interpretive expansion.

---

### v.6 — "sons of disobedience" vs. "all who disobey him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the sons of disobedience" | tous huious tēs apeitheias — Hebraism |
| LOGOS | "the sons of disobedience" | Same — preserves Hebraism |
| PNEUMA | "all who disobey him" | Drops Hebraism; adds "him" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Sons of disobedience" is a Hebraism meaning "those characterized by disobedience." PNEUMA drops the idiom and adds "him" (not in Greek).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA drops Hebraism.

---

### v.16 — "redeeming the time" vs. "making the best use of the time" vs. "make the most of every opportunity"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "redeeming the time" | exagorazomenoi ton kairon — "buying up the opportune time" |
| LOGOS | "making the best use of the time" | Dynamic — captures commercial metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "Make the most of every opportunity in these evil days" | "Every opportunity" — pluralizes; adds "in these evil days" from v.16b |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. exagorazō means "to buy out/redeem" — a commercial metaphor applied to time (kairos, not chronos). LOGOS captures this. PNEUMA pluralizes to "every opportunity" and merges two clauses.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — PNEUMA merges clauses.

---

### v.18 — "be filled in the Spirit" vs. "be filled with the Spirit" vs. "be filled with the Holy Spirit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "be filled in [the] Spirit" | plērousthe en pneumati — locative "in" |
| LOGOS | "be filled with the Spirit" | "With" — instrumental |
| PNEUMA | "be filled with the Holy Spirit" | Adds "Holy" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The preposition en with passive plēroō can be instrumental ("by/with") or locative ("in"). LOGOS renders instrumentally, which fits the wine/Spirit contrast. PNEUMA adds "Holy" — not in the Greek of this verse, though the Holy Spirit is clearly meant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "Holy."

---

### v.26 — "the washing of the water in the word" vs. "by the washing of water with the word" vs. "washed by the cleansing of God's word"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having cleansed [her] by the washing of the water in [the] word" | tō loutrō tou hydatos en rhēmati |
| LOGOS | "having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word" | "With the word" — accompaniment |
| PNEUMA | "washed by the cleansing of God's word" | "God's word" — adds "God's"; drops "water" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek has "the washing of the water in/by [the] word (rhēma)" — baptismal water accompanied by a spoken confession or formula. PNEUMA drops "water" entirely and adds "God's" before "word," losing the baptismal reference.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. The reference to water baptism is explicit in the Greek. PNEUMA's rendering ("the cleansing of God's word") eliminates the sacramental reference, changing a baptismal text into a purely verbal one.

---

### v.27 — "glorious" vs. "in splendor" vs. "a glorious church"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the assembly glorious, not having spot or wrinkle" | endoxon — "glorious/splendid" |
| LOGOS | "the church to himself in splendor" | "In splendor" — adverbial |
| PNEUMA | "a glorious church without a spot or wrinkle or any other blemish" | "Any other blemish" — adds "other" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. endoxon is an adjective — "glorious/splendid." LOGOS renders adverbially ("in splendor"), which works in English. PNEUMA adds "other" before "blemish" — the Greek says "any such thing" (ti toiouton), not "any other blemish."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — PNEUMA adds "other."

---

### v.32 — "This mystery is great" vs. "This mystery is profound" vs. "This is a great mystery, but it is an illustration"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This mystery is great; but I speak concerning Christos and concerning the assembly" | to mystērion touto mega estin |
| LOGOS | "This mystery is profound, and I am saying that it refers to Christ and the church" | "Profound" for mega; "it refers to" |
| PNEUMA | "This is a great mystery, but it is an illustration of the way Christ and the church are one" | "Illustration" — adds; "are one" — adds |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says the mystērion is mega ("great/profound") and that he speaks it with reference to Christ and the church. PNEUMA adds "illustration" and "are one" — Paul does not call the mystery an illustration, nor does he explicitly say "are one" here.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. Paul calls the Genesis text a mystērion — a hidden reality now revealed. PNEUMA's "illustration" downgrades mystery to analogy. Paul's point is that Genesis 2:24 was always about Christ and the church — not merely an illustration of it.

---

### v.33 — "fear" vs. "respect" — phobētai

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the wife, that she fear the husband" | hina phobētai ton andra — "that she fear" |
| LOGOS | "let the wife see that she respects her husband" | "Respects" for phobētai |
| PNEUMA | "the wife must respect her husband" | Same — "respect" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. phobeomai can mean "fear" or "respect/revere." In this spousal context, "respect" captures the relational sense — the same verb is used for reverence toward God (v.21, "reverence for Christ"). EMET's "fear" is lexically accurate but may mislead in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "fear" is literal but potentially misleading.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 3 | "Worshiping the things of this world" (v.5), "Holy" (v.18), "illustration" / "are one" (v.32) |
| PNEUMA drops elements | 2 | "Sons of disobedience" Hebraism (v.6), water/baptism reference (v.26) |
| PNEUMA paraphrase | 1 | "Every opportunity in these evil days" for "redeeming the time" (v.16) |
| Baptismal reference lost | 1 | PNEUMA drops water from v.26, eliminating sacramental reference |
| Mystery downgraded | 1 | PNEUMA calls mystērion an "illustration" (v.32) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 5's most significant disagreements are PNEUMA's elimination of the baptismal water reference (v.26) — changing a sacramental text into a purely verbal one — and the downgrading of mystērion to "illustration" (v.32). Paul's point in v.32 is that Genesis 2:24 was always, in God's design, about Christ and the church — not merely an analogy. PNEUMA's rendering inverts the typological relationship.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
